tweets.text:
China is overtaking Japan as the new powerhouse of the global auto industry:

Chinese automakers now account for a record ~28% of global auto sales.

This percentage has more than DOUBLED since 2019.

Over the same period, Japan’s proportion has declined -7 percentage points, to ~25%, the lowest in at least 10 years.

As a result, China has officially become the world's leader in auto sales.

By 2035, Chinese automakers are expected to account for ~34% of global sales, while Japan's proportion is projected to fall further, to ~22%.

China is taking control of the global car market.

tweets.text:
BREAKING: Foreign US Treasuries held in Fed custody are down to $2.68 trillion, the lowest since 2012.

This measures the value of US government bonds held on behalf of foreign governments, central banks, and other international institutions through accounts at the Federal Reserve.

Since March 2025, Treasury holdings of foreign institutions have declined by -$265 billion.

The drop accelerated since the start of the Iran War, with holdings falling ~$120 billion in just 3 months.

By comparison, Treasuries held in custody at the Fed peaked at $3.14 trillion in March 2021.

This comes as foreign central banks and governments have sold Treasuries to raise US Dollars to prop up their currencies against the inflationary pressures of surging oil prices.

Foreigners are reducing their exposure to US government debt.

tweets.text:
BREAKING: The US Military has conducted new strikes against Iran after Iran launched drones at ships in the Strait of Hormuz, per WSJ.

Details include:

1. US forces shot down Iranian drones and hit a drone-control station at a major port city in southern Iran located on the Strait of Hormuz

2. US officials say the site posed a threat to US forces and commercial traffic in the Strait of Hormuz

3. US F/A-18 jets were used to strike the ground-control unit at the port city in Iran

The strikes come amid crucial US-Iran negotiations.

tweets.text:
The structure of the market is experiencing a historic shift:

The number of US-listed ETFs is up to ~4,900, an all-time high, up +95% since 2020.

Over the same period, the number of US publicly listed companies has declined -1,000, or -20%, to ~3,900, the lowest this century.

As a result, there are now ~1,000 more ETFs than stocks to trade in the US market, the widest gap in history.

Meanwhile, US ETF assets under management (AUM) jumped +10% in April, to a record $14.9 trillion.

Over the last 4 years, US ETF AUM has surged +140% and now accounts for ~69% of the global ETF market.

ETFs are reshaping the foundation of financial markets.

tweets.text:
Wall Street is ramping up hedges against Big Tech:

The total net notional value of credit default swaps (CDS) outstanding on major tech firms is up +$1.0 billion so far in Q2 2026, to a record $12.5 billion.

The total value of debt being insured against default on these companies is up +500% since Q2 2025.

Oracle, $ORCL, leads with ~$6.5 billion, followed by Amazon, $AMZN, at ~$2.0 billion, and Alphabet, $GOOGL, at ~$2.0 billion.

At the same time, Microsoft, $MSFT, stands at ~$1.0 billion, Meta, $META, at ~$800 million, and Nvidia, $NVDA, at ~$200 million.

Furthermore, monthly notional trading volumes of Big Tech CDS trading at Bank of America are up +900% since the start of 2025.

For context, most of these CDS contracts did not trade actively until 2025.

Corporate borrowing tied to AI is exploding.

tweets.text:
US home foreclosures are accelerating:

Properties with foreclosure filings rose +26% YoY in Q1 2026, to ~119,000, the highest in 6 years.

The last time foreclosures were at this high was Q1 2020, just before government relief programs and pandemic stimulus caused them to decline.

Foreclosure filings have more than TRIPLED since the post-pandemic low.

The increase has been driven by soaring home insurance bills, property taxes, and homeowner association fees rather than mortgage defaults alone.

In 2025, the average annual home insurance bill jumped +12% YoY, to a record $2,948.

At the same time, average property tax on US single-family homes rose +3% YoY, to $4,427, also the highest on record.

Financial pressures on homeowners are surging.

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 China is entering its rainy season much earlier than expected, and behind this weather anomaly lies a real economic issue involving agriculture, food security, natural disasters and electricity production.

⛈️ Several Chinese regions are facing rainfall that is earlier, more intense and potentially longer-lasting than usual. Major agricultural provinces such as Henan, Hebei and Anhui are expected to be hit by heavy rainfall between late May and early June.

1️⃣ The first risk is agricultural, as persistent rainfall and insufficient sunshine have already delayed wheat development in parts of Hebei and Henan. Some crops have also been flattened by wind and rain, making harvesting more difficult and increasing the risk of mold. Humidity also favors the spread of diseases and pests, and the real issue is that this is happening at a sensitive moment in the agricultural calendar.

2️⃣ The second issue is disaster management. Some regions have already experienced sudden and deadly floods, particularly in Chongqing. Authorities have announced a 160 million yuan package for prevention and reconstruction, showing that this issue goes well beyond agriculture and also affects infrastructure, transport, civil protection and local public finances.

⚡️ On the other hand, these rains can also support hydropower generation. Hydropower is the country’s second-largest source of electricity, and higher water levels can improve output from major dams. Over the first four months of the year, China’s hydropower generation already reached around 330 billion kWh, up 9.7% year-on-year, a record for the period. At the same time, a cooler and wetter summer in some southern cities could limit air-conditioning use and therefore reduce pressure on electricity demand.

➡ Weather is becoming a real macroeconomic variable, and China is a very clear example of this new reality, where climate change is increasingly reflected in greater instability in seasonal patterns ⚠

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/5nvwh0EdVv

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 #China’s central bank is shifting liquidity management toward balancing funding supply and demand, rather than simply relying on managing the size of injections, Shanghai Securities News reports.

➡ PBOC is now paying more attention to underlying demand for liquidity and would refrain from increasing supply when markets are not short of liquidity.

➡ It would cut back supply when liquidity is ample to avoid pushing rates too low and add funds when needed to prevent conditions from tightening too quickly
It is moving away from quantity-based tools toward greater reliance on price-based measures and expectations guidance.

➡ Going forward, PBOC’s liquidity management is expected to become more precise, flexible and preemptive.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/QjBi2lwh97

tweets.text:
Our regulatory framework is built around companies, and that doesn't fit what blockchains actually are. @milesjennings on the mismatch:

"Our entire regulatory framework from securities laws to corporate law, everything basically assumes that there is a company at the middle that controls and that control persists over time. When you apply that to building networks, right, what it means is that those networks end up being controlled by a centralized company."

"An incredible amount of power accrues to that company at the middle rather than to the people that are actually using that network and the ones that make it valuable."

"Blockchains allow you to remove the company from that and have a network that is fully autonomous on its own. And as a result of that, no one controls it."

@LawofCodeFM

tweets.text: Technological revolutions are often easier to identify in hindsight than in real time. When you're living through them, progress can feel incremental day to day. Then a few years pass and entire workflows, industries, and expectations have quietly changed.

tweets.text:
Crypto bear markets serve an important purpose.

They help correct years of capital misallocation where liquidity and speculation allowed almost anything to go up regardless of fundamentals, sustainability, or actual adoption.

In bear markets, weak narratives fade, excess leverage gets cleared out, and capital slowly rotates back toward stronger projects and more durable infrastructure.

Painful, but necessary.

Painful, but necessary.

tweets.text:
One of the most underappreciated long-term themes is the nuclear industry.

AI growth, electrification, reshoring, and rising global power demand all point to the same thing: the world needs vastly more reliable baseload energy.

For all the debate around markets and cycles, I remain structurally bullish on nuclear over the next decade.

One of the most underappreciated long-term themes is the nuclear industry.

tweets.text:
Countertrend rallies in bear markets often bring out a lot of emotion.

People become anchored to short-term price movements and forget that volatility exists in both directions.

I have no interest in personal attacks or tribalism. My goal has always been to approach markets probabilistically, manage risk, and adapt as new data comes in.

Time will determine which framework best described the cycle.
